     and should not be removed. -->

# Reflection — VoiceForge

> **Note:** This feedback is AI-generated and experimental. It's meant to surface useful observations, not to grade you. Take what resonates, ignore what doesn't. Your own sense of what you learned matters more than anything here.

## Scope & Idea Clarity

**What went well:** The scope doc is one of the tightest produced by this process. "A clean, no-frills web app that converts text to speech and speech to text — no accounts, no database, just the tools working" is one sentence and you completely get the app. "What's Explicitly Cut" lists real features with real reasons — voice cloning, auth, database, multi-language, streaming, diarization — not placeholder text. The "hazlo simple" instinct shows up structurally in the document.

**What to try next time:** Add a concrete, testable success condition to "What Done Looks Like." Something like "a 10-second clip of spoken English generates a complete, accurate transcription" gives you a pass/fail target from day one and sets a sharper anchor for the whole project.

## Requirements Thinking

**What went well:** The PRD shows genuine expansion from scope — the voice selector growing from "one default voice" to four named voices (af_heart, af_bella, am_fenrir, am_michael) is the clearest evidence that the planning conversation surfaced decisions rather than just transcribing them. Acceptance criteria are specific and testable: "If the text box is empty, clicking Generate does nothing (button is unresponsive)" is a criterion you can verify by looking at the screen. Edge cases are addressed: empty input, denied mic permission, unsupported file format, backend failure, PDF extraction failure. That's real requirements thinking.

**What to try next time:** Zero deepening rounds across all phases was a reasonable call given the clarity of the mental model you came in with. But one gap that a deepening round would likely have caught: there's no acceptance criterion for what happens when the Kokoro or Whisper model is still loading at startup and a request comes in. On a self-hosted app with real users, that's a real edge case — worth one round of stress-testing error states.

## Technical Decisions

**What went well:** The spec is the strongest artifact in the set. Every major decision has explicit rationale: "FastAPI + kokoro + faster-whisper keeps the backend pure Python with no external API calls... no vendor lock-in, no usage costs." The file structure is fully annotated. Data flow is diagrammed end-to-end for both TTS and STT paths. The singleton loading decision ("adds ~5-10s to server start time; eliminates per-request latency") demonstrates real tradeoff thinking. Every component maps back to a PRD section — the traceability is thorough.

**What to try next time:** Add a "deployment context" section to the spec before writing component definitions. On this build, ffmpeg/libopus support, Oracle cold-start behavior, and model download timing surfaced late — in the checklist phase and build notes rather than the spec. For a self-hosted app where the infrastructure is part of the product, five lines covering OS, system dependencies, and expected startup constraints would have made the build phase cleaner. (You named this one yourself.)

## Plan vs. Reality

**What went well:** All 10 checklist items completed with zero checklist revisions required. The build notes say it directly: "spec was clean enough to build straight through." Notable technical decisions during the build — WaveSurfer SSR dynamic import, PDF.js worker via CDN to avoid Next.js bundling issues, jsPDF with A4 layout and word-wrapping — were either anticipated in the spec or resolved without plan changes. The build landed on the plan.

**What to try next time:** The build hasn't been fully end-to-end verified yet — model installation and endpoint testing are still pending. On your next project, add a "smoke test" item as the final checklist step: start both servers, hit both endpoints, confirm the core path works end-to-end. That closes the loop before you reflect, and ensures "plan vs. reality" is something you can actually answer.

## How You Worked

**What went well:** You drove the direction throughout. Describing the UI spatially — "abajo a la derecha" for the format selector, "junto al campo" for the mic button — shows you had a concrete picture in your head and were transcribing it, not discovering it through the conversation. Real choices with real reasons: PDF.js over backend pypdf, waveform visualization, ffmpeg on Oracle. The project distinctly reflects your vision. And the "hazlo simple" instinct in response to every scope-cutting question was a clear architectural stance, not passive acceptance.

**What to try next time:** You deferred voice selection and Whisper model size entirely to the agent. On a deployed product with real users, model size is a product decision — it affects the latency and accuracy that users experience. On your next project, when the agent proposes a choice that touches user experience, ask: "Do I have an opinion here?" The goal isn't pushback for its own sake — it's making sure every decision feels like yours, so you can own it when it matters.

## Your Goals

You said you wanted to understand how to work with AI agents effectively — not just use them as autocomplete, but understand the workflow. Both happened. The quiz answers proved it wasn't just a feeling: "A chat window is volatile; a spec file is not" and "a coach makes you describe what you actually need" are insights you'll carry into every project from here. You also got the repeatable process you came for — scope, PRD, spec, checklist, build, reflect. That sequence works whether you're at a hackathon or starting a real project on Monday.

The one thing that would take it further: invest in one deepening round on your next build. Not because your documents were thin — they weren't — but because someone with your experience in a deepening round is stress-testing assumptions, not filling in basics. That's where the real edge cases live.

## Reflection

**What surprised you most about the process?**

> "How little I had to course-correct during the build. I expected the agent to get things wrong — wrong API shape, wrong component structure, wrong assumptions about how pieces connect. That's usually the experience when I jump straight into coding with AI. You spend as much time fixing misunderstandings as you do building.
>
> But because the spec was precise, the agent had almost nothing to guess. The components it built matched the spec's descriptions. The endpoints matched the API contract. The frontend and backend connected on the first try.
>
> The surprise wasn't that the process worked — it was how much of the friction I usually associate with AI-assisted development was actually friction from under-specified context, not from the AI itself.
>
> The tool was never the bottleneck. I was."

That's the insight that takes most people a lot longer to arrive at.
